one_liner: A 68-year-old male with a history of hypertension and obstructive sleep apnea presents with palpitations and lightheadedness for 2 days.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient describes a rapid, regular 'fluttering' sensation in his chest that began 2 days ago. He feels lightheaded when standing and has mild exertional dyspnea. He denies syncope, chest pain, or diaphoresis. He has not been compliant with his CPAP and ran out of his blood pressure medication 1 week ago.
stage2.pmh: Hypertension (10 years), obstructive sleep apnea (moderate, on CPAP), obesity, prediabetes
stage2.surgical_history: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (5 years ago, unsuccessful), right knee arthroscopy
stage2.medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily (ran out 1 week ago), CPAP at night (non-compliant)
stage2.family_history: Mother with atrial fibrillation. Father with type 2 diabetes and stroke at age 72.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. Drinks 2-3 glasses of wine on weekends. Works as an accountant. Sedentary lifestyle.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Alert, mildly anxious, no acute distress. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic with regular rhythm, no murmurs or gallops, normal S1/S2. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Obese, soft, non-tender. Extremities: No edema; Neurological: Alert and oriented, no focal deficits.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Not elevated, no acute myocardial injury
Mildly elevated, may reflect tachycardia-mediated stretch rather than overt HF

imaging_results.result: this condition with 2:1 conduction block, ventricular rate 150 bpm, sawtooth flutter waves best seen in leads II, III, aVF and V1, no ST changes
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Diagnostic of typical this condition with characteristic sawtooth pattern

imaging_results.result: LVEF 55%, no valvular abnormalities, mildly dilated left atrium (4.3 cm), no thrombus visualized, normal RV function
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Preserved systolic function with mild LA dilation consistent with atrial arrhythmia

imaging_results.result: Normal heart size, clear lung fields, no pleural effusion
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No evidence of heart failure or pulmonary pathology

stage3.key_tests_rationale: ECG is essential to identify the rhythm. Troponin rules out ischemia. TSH screens for hyperthyroidism as a reversible cause. BMP checks electrolytes. Echo assesses cardiac structure and function.

stage5.final_clue: The 12-lead ECG reveals a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with classic sawtooth flutter waves in the inferior leads, confirming typical the underlying process with 2:1 conduction.

debrief.classic_presentation: Atrial flutter typically presents with palpitations, lightheadedness, and dyspnea. The ventricular rate is classically ~150 bpm (2:1 block) or ~100 bpm (3:1 block). ECG shows sawtooth flutter waves at ~300/min, best seen in leads II, III, aVF. Risk factors include hypertension, obesity, OSA, and structural heart disease.
debrief.pathophysiology: Typical atrial flutter is a macro-reentrant circuit in the right atrium, traveling counterclockwise around the tricuspid valve annulus through the cavotricuspid isthmus. The atrial rate is ~300 bpm, and the AV node provides conduction block (usually 2:1), resulting in a ventricular rate of ~150 bpm. Unlike atrial fibrillation, flutter is an organized rhythm.
debrief.treatment_overview: Rate control: IV diltiazem or beta-blocker to slow ventricular response. Cardioversion: electrical cardioversion is highly effective (often at low energy 50-100J). Anticoagulation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score determines stroke risk (same as AFib). Definitive therapy: catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus has >90% success rate and is first-line for recurrent flutter.
high_yield_pearls: A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm should raise strong suspicion for atrial flutter with 2:1 block.
Sawtooth flutter waves are best seen in leads II, III, aVF and may be revealed by adenosine (which increases AV block transiently).
Atrial flutter carries similar stroke risk to atrial fibrillation — anticoagulation decisions follow CHA₂DS₂-VASc scoring.
Catheter ablation of the cavotricuspid isthmus is curative in >90% of typical atrial flutter and is preferred over long-term antiarrhythmic drugs.
Obstructive sleep apnea is a significant and modifiable risk factor for atrial flutter and fibrillation.
sources: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation — https://www.heart.org/en/professional/quality-improvement/guidelines-and-statements
Granada et al., Typical Atrial Flutter, Circulation 2000 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
